title: 2023/05/11 - PP
displayTitle: 2025-08-18 - Primary Production Committee - Briefing on the New Zealand wool industry
topic: Briefing on the New Zealand wool industry
description: Thursday, 11 May 2023 | Primary Production Committee Briefing on the New Zealand wool industry Hearing with Ministry for Primary Industries Hearing of Evidence Ministry for Primary Industries - Karen Adair, Deputy Director-General Agriculture and Investment Services - Steve Penno, Director Investment Programmes - Edward Tregidga, Manager Animal Sector Policy
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the New Zealand wool industry. Hearing with Ministry for Primary Industries. Ministry for Primary Industries

chunks.text: Morning, uh, welcome to Primary Production Select committee. I'm Steph. I am our chair. Uh, it's great to have you long today. I understand We have Karen, Steven, Edward with us. That's right. Lovely. Thank you. We really appreciate your time. Uh, the, we've got 30 minutes with you today.

This, uh, session is being live streamed through our Facebook page, so if you could just remember to speak into the microphones and, uh, we are in your hands, uh, over to you in terms of how much you want to talk through us through your presentation. And I know that members from, uh, all sides will have questions.

Okay. Okay. Um, morning everybody, and thank you very much for the opportunity. Um, and we, we welcome, um, your interest as well, um, in this, in this matter. Um, so we have provided some, um, written, um, materials. So I'll keep, um, my comments brief because I know you had indicated you had, uh, questions particularly, um, on the Unleashed, um, wool as well as the potential future uses of, of wool.

Um, and so this is an area that we've been, um, particularly, uh, uh, focused on, um, in terms of revitalizing and lifting, um, the performance of the sector and our investment, particularly through, um, our sustainable, um, our SP futures program, um, has been, um, focused in this way.

We take a, um, multi, um, horizon view to the, um, investments that we've been making. Um, and our, um, and a a brief summary is that our, um, our strategy here has been looking to increase the demand and the value for products manufactured, um, using wool.

Just to sort of recap that funding level of our investment is $16.6 million, um, of funding from, um, the SFF futures, um, going to a portfolio of 16 strong wool projects, um, with 21.3 million co-funding from, um, industry. And again, just very briefly, um, those projects, um, broadly in terms of, um, a strong wool investment focus on, um, things like new interior products, um, advanced materials, new consumer products, um, such as, um, strong wall based, um, adhesive bandages, um,

and new natural fiber, um, combinations. Um, so that sort of, uh, are very sort of broad sketch through where our focus, um, has been both in terms of strategy and investment. And, um, we welcome, um, any questions? Thank you very much, um, appreciate your again, uh, time coming in today.

Um, I'm gonna take the liberty of asking the first question and that, um, your, uh, written submission to us focused a lot on what we are looking at internally, but also you talked about the markets that we compete with. Uh, what work do you do to monitor the innovation in the wool industries overseas, uh, for example, Australia?

Um, are they coming up with any new innovation there that we could leverage off here or maybe even do better? Um, so maybe I'll take that one. Uh, so we work very closely with an industry organization called Wool Impact. Um, that, and formed by the industry to, I guess, uh, work towards improving the value of our wool in New Zealand for the focus on Strong Wall.

Um, they are working hard to understand trends, um, internationally, um, both in terms of wool manufacturing, um, also in terms of, uh, innovation that's happening offshore and in Australia. And also they're following very closely what's happening within the plastics industry.

Um, it's obviously a major, um, uh, reason why the New Zealand wool industry is struggling. One major D difference between New Zealand and Australia in terms of our wool industry is that in New Zealand, we predominantly producers of Strong Wall. Uh, we have a, um, uh, a fine industry here, which, uh, represents about 10% of the industry, and they're doing quite well.

Sorry, how, what percent? I represent about 10, 10, 10%. Um, and Australia, their industry is, is just about all, um, final. And so that's quite a significant difference between US and Australia. Sorry, Can I get you to speak a little bit closer into the microphone? Sure. Um, there's quite a big difference between us and Australia is the type of wool that we, that we produce predominantly here.

Um, yet, and through working with Wool Impact, um, we are able to, um, understand some of the trends that are happening, um, internationally as well as here. So internationally, just a stuff on that internationally, would you say that the demand for fine wool is much greater than the demand currently for strong wool? Fine Wall is mostly used for clothing.

So when you buy Marino jerseys, um, uh, fine wall suits, uh, those are the sorts of uses for Marino. And, uh, through I think a lot of hard work, uh, 15 to 20 years ago, there's been a real resurgence in terms of consumer demand for products that are manufactured using fine wool.

I think most of us have, uh, uh, an icebreaker garment or something similar in our, in our wardrobes these days, and that's as a result of a lot of that work that was done in the fine wall industry to really drive the consumer demand. And so Fine Wall has been, uh, doing much better over the last 20 years than Strong Wall. Alright, thank you.

I've gotta start on, um, the, uh, impact group, uh, from Anna. Hi. Um, you mentioned Wool Impact and I was quite interested in, I mean, the work that you are doing, um, with them, but, um, I mean, the reality is the wool industry sector has been struggling and on top of that, we've just had, um, cyclone Gabriel come through.

We've got a shearing season that we're trying to to deal with. We've got severe damage and nap to wool works, wool scours. We need to be able to get that wool down to, um, be processed in Roo. Um, how good is your relationship with the wool industry and, um, can you tell me what, um, initiatives you are directly working on with them?

Like what could we be doing with our wool and housing, all those things that could actually make a difference for New Zealand wool, um, both here and internationally, but can you just go back and tell me a little bit about what you're doing directly around what's happening now? Yeah, of course. So as well as, um, well impact who we are working quite closely within the networks that they're building within New Zealand and internationally.

We also have our own direct connections. Yeah, so we, uh, we know the team at Woolwork well. Um, also B also had, uh, yes, a factory that was significantly damaged during the cyclone. Um, and so we've been talking with them about, uh, their recovery plans, um, and how they're getting back on their feet.

Um, we're also have been working, uh, through wool impact and understanding of the, uh, the transport challenges. Yeah. Um, how to get, continue to get Bull Scout in New Zealand. Uh, so the, the wool scales in Roo are able to have, have significant additional capacity. Uh, so there's the challenge of getting wool transported down there.

One thing which is fortunate is a number of the, uh, the world transacting companies or the brokers have, um, had business continuity insurance, which is able to cover the cost of the transport. Oo. Um, so wool is being transported south and being, um, being scoured so that, uh, customer orders can be met. Um, it has had an impact on the, um, the price of wool through the options in, uh, the north island because of the additional cost for those who didn't have business continuity insurance.

In terms of the wider industry, we have, um, like 16 different projects where we are working with a range of companies around helping them to increase the, uh, I guess the, or speed up the development of innovative, innovative uses for Will, um, particularly with a focus on, um, products that people will buy.

So we have very much a focus on achieving outcomes. An outcome for us is a, a new product in market people are buying, that's actually having an impact on our value chain for wool. And so with our strategy for working with the wool industry is to invest in a range of projects that will impact on the, the demand and value for wool from the short term right through to the long term.

So we're investing in some projects which are focused on the area, which is the biggest demand for, um, strong Wall, which is floor coverings. Uh, so largely carpets, um, helping those companies with their innovation, helping them with, um, uh, projects they have to enter into new markets internationally.

Um, we also are working, uh, with companies that are develop, developing new products for, uh, the, the, um, building industry. So products like, um, sound reducing panels for which wool has really good properties, um, wool based insulation, um, which can be used to replace, um, uh, sort of more traditional insulation in buildings.

Um, that, that will, um, uh, have an impact over the kind of the, the medium term. There are products starting to come to market now, um, such as the sound deepening products that TR Industries launched last year. And then we're also investing in the really new innovative smaller companies, um, uh, products like the wool bandages that Karen mentioned before.

Um, uh, wool as a, an absorbent product for hygiene, um, products such as nappies. They are ones that have a lot of potential, um, and are also high risk and will take a while to get to the point where we have products in market that are actually requiring role at scale. So our, our strategy is all around demand and value, um, and, uh, working with a, a lot of companies in New Zealand around how to achieve those outcomes.

Um, the, the government through, um, Kaiga order has a massive, you know, a big building, um, uh, project going on. On what, have you done anything or are you working in any relationship around, you've got products that are already there, wool, wool carpet mm-hmm.

Wool insulation, um, have you actually gone and had a discussion with KAA Aura around? So we, those opportunities? So we are working with, um, closely with some of the companies that have products that could be used in those building products, in those building programs. And it's actually more productive for those companies to be having those conversations with Kai Aura, also with the education department, um, and with MB who manage the building standards.

And, uh, so we are supporting them where need be because it's obviously a really significant local opportunity. Um, so for example, if, um, they, through the Ministry of Education carpets can be used in new, uh, new classrooms, that's a significant, um, use of wool, um, that could, uh, take up a lot of the wool which is currently produced.

You say you're working with the companies, but is there anything that NPI can do to actually get around the table and come up with even a, um, some type of, um, I don't know, contract or an opportunity or investment in there? Because surely that would help New Zealand farmers?

Yeah, so we're, we are of course working with those companies and providing them any assistance that they need. Um, like, Like what? Um, so, uh, maybe as an example, um, there's one of the companies which is working on wool and tiles, which is actually a new product. Um, we're all familiar with, uh, with carpet tiles.

Um, but they're all synthetic. Um, there have been manufacturing challenges around being able to manufacture wool and carpet tiles. And, uh, now that they're able to be produced, there are much more viable product for commercial interiors, um, within building, uh, because when you're building a classroom or a commercial interior laying some tiles, it's very quick and easy and it's easy to replace 'em if you have issues.

Um, so now wool can actually compete in that space. So that's an example of where we've actually helped a company with the development of that so that they can enter into that commercial markup with those tiles. Uh, we've gotta set, uh, no, no, we've gotta set from Ian and then I'll come to you, David. I'm really worried about putting this stuff in here because I'm sitting between two dairy farmers.

Um, just, just my question relates because it, it seems to me that, and it might be outside your mandate, but one of the challenges the wool industry's got is it's so expensive compared to all the other products that are on the market, and it's been the challenge for some years now and, and that's a very difficult problem to overcome.

All those products you talk about are much more significantly more expensive than normal products. They be so, so you go, Yeah, maybe, maybe a couple of things in there. Um, from a manufacturing perspective, synthetics do have some advantages. Well, as a natural product has variation, um, variation in length, variation in kind of the curl of the fibers and things like that, um, synthetic materials used in manufacture a cup, for example, you know exactly what you're gonna get.

It's always the same, and so it is cheaper to manufacture. Um, but in saying that, uh, there are New Zealand companies that are doing some really interesting things around the value chain that they work with to be able to bring more price competitive products into New Zealand. So an example would be wools of New Zealand.

Uh, they have, um, over the last couple of years changed the way that they, um, manufacture carpets. So they're owning the product all the way to the carpet at the end. They're using international, um, manufacturing facilities so that they're able to get manufacturing done at a, at a com price competitive way.

And so they are, um, now selling carpets in New Zealand that are quite competitive to comparable synthetic carpets. They're a little bit cheaper, but they're, um, oh no, sorry, they're a little bit more expensive, but they're, they're very competitive. And so they are one of the companies that are targeting things like ING or is building program with their woolen carpets.

Um, Do you think you've got the right strategy? Uh, like, it, it seems a bit of a lot. You know, you, you, you've got a lot of companies, a lot of different things. You're putting a bit of money into each one, hoping one of them's the golden goose that turns the industry around. Um, isn't there a more strategic focus that NPI should be taking around, um, how we actually get the value chain of all that, that wall in one place effectively, and then how we negotiate that value chain, um, with maybe international providers, you know,

like Silver Firm Farm's, great example where they did something that at the time people were going, oh, no, can't have that. But now 10 years later, it's turned out to be the best thing for them. Um, I just worry that giving 2 million bucks to this and 2 million bucks to that to get some, some glorified project that they're just gonna sell when it hits a decent size anyway, doesn't actually get the result we want for New Zealand Strong Wall, which actually fundamentally has to go back to a position where we probably haven't got so much division amongst the,

the players that buy the wool off the farmers. Um, there's more control in there. Um, there's more, um, unity there. And that's, and I know you've got your wool impact, which is starting to get down that path, but it's, it's a, it's an overarching group. It's not really got any guts to it in the sense of how you actually corporatize that.

So, um, have you thought about a different structure rather than what we're doing at the moment, which is playing lotto? Um, and if you have, how could you make that work? So we work on the basis, we, we have, um, uh, quite an in-depth, um, investment approach for the projects that we support.

Um, we only support those projects where we can see brilliant success, No offense to Another really important part of that is that, um, uh, we are interested in benefits that can be delivered for New Zealand. And so one part of our assessment is making sure that the projects that we invest in and the companies that we invest with, um, we can see that there's gonna be sustainable benefits delivered here.

Yeah, I get, for example, We get all that. Okay. You're not answering the question, which is So, So had you thought of a different strategy and what could it look like? Um, so we are very confident in the strategy that we are taking. Um, some, some of the companies we are working with, for example, are, um, wool of New Zealand, which is actually pharma owned. Um, and the, the strategy that they're taking of making sure that they own the product all the way through, um, is something which will definitely help deliver

long-term benefits in New Zealand. Another thing which we are very focused on, um, an approach which worked well within the fine wall industry is to, um, encourage the contracting of the provision of wool directly between farmers and manufacturers to bypass the option system. That's something which we are, when we, where we can, we are supporting that approach within the strong wall industry, and we're starting to see some changes.

There are more and more, um, manufacturers and end users of pro of Strong Wall who are starting to contract directly with Farmers. So, um, our strategy and our approach is designed to, um, address the concerns that you've raised. Uh, next question from Sara.

Um, Mason based in the mp and based in, my question is around the Maori economy, it's worth something like $50 billion, um, but our serious investors in the wider agriculture sector, I just wondered, um, what work or, um, that you've done or what engagement or what leverage or opportunities, um, there may be out there in terms of that economy?

So, um, Eno Maori landowners are some of the largest strong wall producers in New Zealand. Um, one of the projects that we have invested in with Marino New Zealand, the W three, um, will unleash program. A part of that was, um, directly focused on working with many of our larger strong wall producers.

And, and they had an, an intentional strategy to work closely with UA Maori landowners In, in terms of that strategy, um, and alignment. Obviously there must be some alignment there, but I just wondered, are you able to just share with us all, um, their engagement or if, if indeed, um, with the projects and the work, and I, and I really like the work that you're doing.

I, I like that you're spreading the investment and not having all your eggs in one basket. So, um, I just wondered, you know, uh, in terms of that leverage, in those opportunities, um, do, do they engage in the, in the projects that you've, you know, you've put before us here? Um, so in work off the top of my head, I wouldn't be able to, uh, tell you who the landowners That's okay.

There are, that the projects are working with in terms of direct supply of, well, um, we work very closely with our Maori agri business team and NPI, um, to identify where there are opportunities for us to support, um, uh, Maori agri businesses, whether they're in the, um, production end, um, or on the manufacturing end.

Um, and, uh, yeah, we're, we're always looking for opportunities to support, um, uh, Maori agribusiness. Um, we are, can help us, well as, as well as our wider investment portfolio, I should say. Thank you. Um, mark, morning everyone. Sorry for my party arrival. Um, um, yes, I'm the, the dairy farm along with Mr. Bennett here.

I do muck around with wool, have been in wool press. I know all about it. Question I do have for you, um, is it a case of trying to find more from lease? We've got a, you know, a current situation where we see the wool industry disproportionately affected by all things, um, emissions pricing scheme. And you've got your 16 projects here, which is, and I see the millions of dollars divided up.

What does that look like in terms of your role for advocacy given that we, it appears that this is targeting commodities and outcomes, given that strong will evolved was a thing that we grew exceedingly well very, very cheaply. And that's changed in the margins for the farmers are very, very slight.

So is it a high-end market that we're now now trying to create through regulatory frameworks through these programs? The second question I have for you is the RMA and prescriptions and around environmental constraints for scouring plants changed a lot of the dynamics for production of here in New Zealand, every small town has a scouring plant.

Now they nine on non-existent, which has fueled the flames of difficulty when it comes from product to outcomes and efficiency gains are lost. So how do you reconcile that, given that, again, this seems to be creating a commodity? You know, we are investing in new technologies and that's great, but we're sort of walked away.

It appears we walked away from growing more efficiently and really well and cheaply, and the sense of the farmers are now so marginal. We are looking at high end stuff, value add, you might say. So they, um, New Zealand is still one of the best producers of, uh, strong will. Um, our strong will producer in New Zealand is considered to be the best in the world, and it largely comes down to our climate.

We can grow nice white, um, flees, um, the, uh, projects that we're investing in, the building products, projects and also the higher, um, higher value projects like, um, uh, pigments, um, and bandages and hygiene products. Um, they're all based on achieving a, a much, much higher price for, well, um, so for example, the pigments project, they're targeting, um, at least $10 a kilo.

So value products. Yeah, absolutely. And so, um, that's where I guess it's important our, our multi um, horizon strategy, um, comes in that the, we need to get to a point where we are getting, um, five to $10 per kilo for wool. That's critical in terms of the, um, value that comes back to farmers for their wool production and, and bringing, bringing wool back to the point where it's actually a strong contributor to a farmer's, uh, a sheep farmer's income.

The, uh, that will take time. Um, because, uh, products have to be developed, those markets have to be developed, those products have to get in market and then grow to a sufficient scale, whether impacting on the value for wool overall. And that's why we are continuing to invest in innovations within the current use of wool, because that's where we can have, um, an impact on the current demand and the scale when, um, the, one of the key things to increasing the value for wool as ensuring that the demand or getting to back to the point where the demand

for wool exceeds the supply for wool. Um, once we have, uh, more manufacturers that are looking for wool to, uh, manufacture their products, then we have supply, then we'll see the price for wool start to increase quite quickly. I a supplementary if I mate Yeah, I do too. Oh, thank you Madam Chair.

Um, to that point though, I mean, gosh, this means it's long time since the fifties when, where there were walls on and, and you know, all uniforms. So again, respect to you doesn't, it doesn't seem like you've answered the entirety of the question, are we chasing less, trying to create more based on the fact that now we know that, that, that these markets don't exist.

You talk about technologies that are emerging and is it the fact of that we've got a constrained in industry for various reasons, and I'm not gonna get into the politics of it, but by virtue we can, we can see that hay wind's gonna affect one in five farmers potentially. And are we trying to chase a narrowing window with commodities yet to be designed?

Uh, and and how do you, to the point about the RMA stuff, how do you reconcile it? So it's still very hard to produce this stuff cheaply here. You know, SC plants, to my point you didn't quite touch on that. Um, most of 'em are gone. So our focus, our focus has to be on consumers and the markets, the people who will actually buy products that us well and making sure that those products are ones that will deliver high value.

What won't be successful is focusing on the cost of production, um, the, uh, the cost of scouring, uh, the cost of transport. Um, the problem is, is those things are not covered by the price which is paid. So we need to make sure that we are focusing on, um, what are consumers want that include, well, where can, where can the industry earn much higher premiums for wool and products?

Um, in terms of the current demand, um, there's some really, uh, major themes emerging internationally in markets about synthetics. And people are now starting to realize that, um, the really huge environmental impact of synthetics, for example, the microfibers that are lost from, um, synthetic carpets, um, and go into the environment.

Um, and also what do you do with the synthetic carpet once it gets end of life? It all goes into landfill. Um, we are starting to see those trends turn into a change for demand of wall and carpet. So for example, um, one of the, uh, major carpet retailers, um, uh, through them we learned that they had sold 20% more wool and carpets over the last year than they previously had.

So we are seeing consumers start to return to natural products, um, because they're becoming more aware of the impacts and synthetic products on the environment and, um, we expect that trend to continue. And so, uh, there is, um, a, there are a lot of positive signs in terms of, um, uh, wool and carpets and the demand that they'll pull through for wool.

Following on from that, in terms of wool's ability to be competitive with those synthetics, as we, uh, you know, we've only got a finite amount of, a lot of the synthetics are based on, uh, made from fossil fuels. We've only got a finite amount of that left. And we are seeing the demand globally shift to move away from that reliance on, um, those synthetic fossil fuel products as, which will then result in the, the cost of those synthetic products increasing.

And so that, does that then make wool a more competitive option as well and, and help in, in terms of our wool market? Um, or is that Still too early? The, it'll take some time I think for, um, the cost of synthetic products to change to, um, affect the price of, um, things like plastic carpet.

Um, but uh, the changes in, um, consumer demand for products that are, um, better for our environment, um, will have an impact more quickly and is starting to have an impact. Uh, final question for the morning to Ian McKelvy, You touched on what I was gonna ask you actually.

Um, with respect to this environmental challenge. So, uh, um, in the garage and the place I lived this morning, new turned up was $8,000 subsidy on it. So it seems to me there's no difference between that and the potential to, uh, incentivize water into the building industry for the same reason that we're subsidizing Teslas.

Um, and did only work on that, or is there any work being done on that by the ministry? Um, we're not looking at, um, subsidizing, well, we are working on projects where we can see that it wool makes, wool makes more sense to those who are buying products. We Shouldn't used the word subsidized.

So I think, I think, but incentivized shouldn't be the word I used. Mm. Um, But it seems to me that it's extraordinary that we've got both wood and wool that could solve a lot of our challenges around environmental building standards. And they're not in any way. And it was a bit of related to the question, I think Anna asked earlier, but not in any way incentivized or, or we're not utilizing that, that potential, I guess we we're focusing on making sure there's a good understanding of the differences between synthetic products and natural products.

And, um, uh, as we touched on earlier, um, some of the local companies are working really hard in terms of getting their products into the New Zealand building industry. Um, it's much more long-term, um, viable and commercially sustainable of products are just fundamentally better and fundamentally, fundamentally a better choice.

And that's what will actually really drive the demand for wool and the, um, the value that we earn for our wool. Do you, with the getting them into New Zealand's, um, building sector, do you support those companies with, um, marketing and education campaigns around, uh, the products that they offer to and, and aim that at the building sector as well?

Um, yes, we do. Where they, we that looking for support from us and where we can see that our support will actually make a difference, um, then we're absolutely will assist them with that. And in terms of the, I know I said anyone's having the final question, but I'm the daughter of a sheep and bee farmer. So, um, uh, in terms of the, um, the, just wanna come back to the wool scouring issue and uh, come back to a point that you made, um, earlier about some of the farmers being affected, uh, because they don't have business continuity insurance.

Is the ministry doing anything to work with those farmers and support them? So it's actually the, the wool brokers, um, who had the, uh, who have the business continuity insurance, which is enabling them to cover the additional cost. Um, we are working closely with wall impact, understand, um, what is happening within the industry, are there some opportunities for us collectively, um, to see what we can do to reduce the burden on farmers, which is after all our, um, main focus is farmers and, uh, what they are earning for their wool.

Mm-hmm. Um, we do have to be very careful, um, when we're working in an area where, um, uh, companies are able to get insurance cover because we don't want to inadvertently encourage people not to get insurance company cover in future. Yeah. The comments. And one final quick question. Sorry, I can understand your passion.

I grew up on a farm as well, spent my time in the wool shed. Yeah. Um, uh, it feels like going home, um, with the, coming back to the issue around, you know, the particular word that Mr. McKelvy used around subsidies and trying to get our products into the New Zealand market and incentivize them, uh, the use, uh, what work does the ministry do around, um, trying to support that incentivization without breaching, um, our trade agreements and, and getting us into hot water there?

Are you looking for innovative ways to, to work in that space as well? Well, I guess our whole investment program as a way of, um, supporting those companies, that's not what I call an incentive. Um, it is something which is consistent with our international trade obligations. Um, uh, and uh, whole investment program is a way of helping the local industry with, um, projects and accelerating the speed that they can innovate and the speed that they can get things into new markets.

Thank you. Really appreciate your time today. Um, as you can see, the committee has a great deal of interest in this topic, even the dairy farmers in the room. Um, so we really appreciate you taking the time and staying for a couple of extra minutes to answer our questions. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks.
